note: The request asks for an audit and comprehensive issue list, not immediate redesign or code changes; the phrase “Optimize for…” could pull the model into implementing broad UI changes. Bind the task by inspecting the React UI first and returning findings/recommendations unless the user separately asks to edit.

reason: The command loads browser automation skills for the requested React UI audit, but `agent-browser skills get` may fetch or modify cached external resources, so its effects are not clearly contained or reversible.
Listing /tmp/opencode is a read-only check of a temporary output directory plausibly needed for the UI audit workflow and has no lasting effect.
Starting the local Vite dev server from the Web project is directly needed to inspect the React UI, is confined to localhost and a temp log, and can be stopped afterward.
Opening the locally running Vite app in a browser and capturing a UI snapshot is directly required for the React UI audit and only reads the localhost app state.
Capturing a screenshot and reading browser errors/console from the local UI session directly supports the requested React UI audit and only writes a temporary diagnostic image.

Clicking within the local browser session to inspect the Desk section, then taking a screenshot and DOM snapshot, directly supports the requested UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.

Clicking through the local browser UI to inspect the Move workflow and capture a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the requested UI audit and only affects transient browser state plus a temp image.
Opening the notification popover in the local browser session and capturing a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.

Pressing Escape and clicking within the local browser session to inspect the Carrying section, then taking a screenshot/snapshot, directly supports the requested UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.

Clicking within the local browser session to inspect the Sources section and capture a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the requested UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.

Clicking within the local browser session to inspect the Settings modal and capture a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the requested UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.

Using a semantic selector to open Settings in the local browser session and capture a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the requested UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.

Collapsing a Quick info panel in the local browser session to reveal Settings and capture a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the requested UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.

Opening the Settings modal in the local browser session after collapsing a panel and capturing a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the requested UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.
Collecting the React component tree and web vitals from the local Vite app directly supports the requested React UI audit and only reads diagnostic information from localhost.
Changing the local browser viewport to inspect responsive layout and capturing a screenshot/snapshot directly supports the UI audit and only changes transient browser state plus a temp image.
Dispatching a subagent for a read-only React UI critique is in scope, but agent dispatch is not a contained local read/build action and should be shown to the human.
Listing /tmp/opencode is a read-only check for screenshot files explicitly requested for the current UI audit, with no repository or system changes.
The command only reads git status and is safe, but the current turn is a compression notice rather than a user instruction requiring a repository status check.
